summary: Inspired by Tim Bray's resignation from Amazon, DeVault argues that software engineers are morally complicit in the harmful actions of their employers. He contends that tech workers enjoy exceptional job mobility and high salaries, which eliminates the excuse of being 'trapped' at unethical companies. He catalogs the sins of major tech companies — surveillance, labor exploitation, contempt for law and open standards — and argues that each employee's economic contribution directly enables these harms. The post warns that even well-intentioned engineers can be gradually co-opted through interesting technical work that serves harmful ends, and calls on developers to make deliberate moral choices about where they apply their enormous productive power.
key_points: Software engineers have exceptional job security and mobility, removing the excuse of being trapped at unethical employers
Your employer profits at least 1.5x your salary from your work, making your individual contribution to their harmful activities economically significant
Major tech companies are collectively responsible for mass surveillance, labor exploitation, tax evasion, and attacks on open standards
Engineers can be gradually co-opted through exciting technical projects that are later applied to harmful purposes, with cognitive dissonance protecting the ego
The massive productivity multiplier of software development creates an outsized moral responsibility for how that power is used
money_quotes: In tech, hardly anyone is "trapped" at a bad job; or at least we don't have a good excuse for not trying for something better.
Mass surveillance, contempt of the law, tax evasion, oppression of the poor, of minorities… this is what our industry is known for, and it's our fault.
If the best defense we have for working at these companies is the Nuremberg defense, that doesn't reflect well on us.
Doublethink quickly steps in to protect your ego from the cognitive dissonance, and you take another little step towards becoming the person you once swore never to be.
When you're making a cool half-million annual salary from $bigcorp, it's because they expect to make at least ¾ of a million that they wouldn't be making without you.
Every software developer possesses alone the potential of 50 engineers living just 100 years ago.
We can apply this power for good or for ill, but it's up to each of us to make a deliberate choice on the matter.
key_insight: Software engineers have both the economic privilege to leave unethical employers and the moral obligation to do so, because their outsized productive power makes them directly complicit in the harms those companies cause.
